metadata.title: Differentiation Audit: Preserving the Intellectual Target
metadata.description_blurb: The most dangerous differentiation error is invisible: Claude simplifies the task so thoroughly that the student completes it without doing the thinking — and the teacher never notices because the work looks finished.

beats.narration_text: This is Kore for Humanitarians AI.

props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · Differentiation Audit: Preserving the Intellectual Target

beats.narration_text: The most dangerous differentiation error is invisible. The adapted assignment looks simpler — it is. The student completes it — they do. The work looks finished — it does. But the student never did the thinking the assignment was designed to produce, because Claude simplified the task instead of the language. The teacher does not know. This is Kore, in for Humanitarians AI.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, plain-language annotation assignment

props.command: Create a plain-language version of this primary source annotation assignment for students reading two grade levels below

beats.narration_text: Here is why Differentiation Audit: Preserving the Intellectual Target belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.

The learning target is non-negotiable. Simpler language is the goal. Simpler intellectual demand is the failure. The five-question audit checks whether the target survived. Question one: is the learning target preserved? Simpler vocabulary for the same analytic demand — pass. Simpler vocabulary and a simpler task — fail.
beats.new_visual_element: Target preserved check — simpler language vs simpler demand

beats.narration_text: Claude will sometimes flag its own adaptation. 'The word assumptions may still need defining for students at this level.' When Claude flags, use it. That flag is a search signal pointing at the exact place where the adaptation may have shifted from language to task. The flag is not a warning to dismiss — it is a question to answer.
beats.new_visual_element: Claude's flag — use the hedged output as a search signal

beats.narration_text: Audit question three: does any part of the adapted assignment do the student's thinking for them? If the original asked students to identify the author's claim and evaluate the evidence, and the adapted version provides the claim and asks students to circle the evidence — the thinking has moved from the student to the prompt. That is not differentiation. That is substitution.
beats.new_visual_element: Audit Q3 — does any part do the student's thinking?

beats.narration_text: Five audit questions, all of them teacher-review questions that Claude cannot answer on your behalf. Is the target preserved? Is the demand equivalent? Does any part do the student's thinking? Is the adaptation offered with dignity? Are all paths leading to the same learning outcome? Fluent adaptation aligned to nobody is still the failure.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — five-question differentiation audit checklist

beats.narration_text: Run the audit on your next differentiated assignment. Paste this.

props.command: Here is the original assignment and the adapted version. Run the differentiation audit: (1) Is the learning target preserved? (2) Is the intellectual demand equivalent? (3) Does any part of the adaptation do the student's thinking for them? (4) Is the simplification in language only, or also in task? (5) Flag any place where the adaptation may have crossed from support into substitution. [paste both versions]

beats.narration_text: If this catches an invisible failure before the lesson, subscribe. Kore, in for Humanitarians AI. From Humanitarians AI.
